questions.whyCorrect: Elevated serum ACE level is found in approximately 60% of patients with active sarcoidosis and supports the diagnosis in the appropriate clinical context. While not specific enough to be diagnostic alone, it is the most supportive laboratory finding listed.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: The presentation is classic for pulmonary sarcoidosis: bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, restrictive pattern with reduced DLCO, and elevated ACE. CT chest provides detailed staging and transbronchial biopsy confirms non-caseating granulomas while ruling out infection and malignancy.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: Painless lymphadenopathy with B symptoms (night sweats, weight loss, fever) and pruritus in a young adult is classic for Hodgkin lymphoma. Excisional biopsy is the gold standard, as it preserves nodal architecture necessary for diagnosis and subtyping (Reed-Sternberg cells in proper microenvironment).
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: The clinical picture, including severe hyperglycemia, metabolic acidosis with an anion gap, but absence of significant ketones suggests Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State (HHS), which is more common in type 2 diabetes. DKA (A) typically presents with ketonuria and significant ketosis.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: This is an acute exacerbation of COPD (AECOPD) with increased dyspnea, sputum volume, and purulence (Anthonisen type I). ABG shows acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis (compensated chronic hypercapnia with acute worsening).
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: Respiratory acidosis is indicated by a decreased pH and increased PaCO2 due to inadequate ventilation. An increased pH with decreased PaCO2 indicates respiratory alkalosis, while normal pH with increased bicarbonate suggests metabolic compensation.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: The ABG results indicate a low pH (7.32), elevated PaCO2 (58 mmHg), and HCO3 (30 mEq/L), which reflects acute respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation. In COPD patients, the high PaCO2 indicates hypoventilation, leading to respiratory acidosis.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: This is new-onset type 1 diabetes presenting with diabetic ketoacidosis (hyperglycaemia, metabolic acidosis pH <7.3, ketonemia). In paediatric DKA management, fluid resuscitation is the first priority before insulin.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: Tamsulosin, an alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist, is commonly used to relieve urinary symptoms associated with BPH by relaxing smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck. Finasteride is used to shrink the prostate but takes longer to see effects.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: Bacterial vaginosis is diagnosed by Amsel criteria (3 of 4: thin gray discharge, clue cells, positive whiff test, pH greater than 4.5). First-line treatment is metronidazole 500 mg orally twice daily for 7 days or metronidazole gel intravaginally.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: Acute urinary retention with 650 mL PVR requires immediate bladder decompression via catheterization to prevent bladder injury and post-obstructive renal damage. Diphenhydramine (anticholinergic) likely precipitated the retention by decreasing detrusor muscle contractility in a patient with pre-existing BPH.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: This presentation is classic Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP): severely immunocompromised HIV patient (CD4 < 200), subacute onset of dyspnoea with dry cough, bilateral interstitial infiltrates, elevated LDH, and significant hypoxaemia. Adjunctive corticosteroids (prednisone 40 mg twice daily for 5 days, then taper) are indicated when PaO2 < 70 mmHg or SpO2 < 92% on room air, as they reduce mortality.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin, doxazosin) are first-line pharmacological treatment for moderate BPH symptoms. They relax smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck, improving urinary flow within days.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: Lytic bone lesions in a patient with a history of breast cancer are highly suspicious for metastatic disease. A bone scan evaluates the extent of skeletal involvement, and urgent oncology referral is required for staging and treatment planning.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

questions.whyCorrect: Acute COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis and hypoxemia requires controlled oxygen therapy (target 88-92% to avoid suppressing hypoxic respiratory drive), systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40 mg for 5 days), and antibiotics (if increased sputum purulence suggests infection). High-flow oxygen can worsen CO2 retention in COPD patients.
questions.whyWrong: Detailed distractor explanations are not available for this item yet.
questions.clinicalPearl: Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.

flashcards.whyCorrect: Respiratory = Opposite (pH and CO2 go opposite ways). Metabolic = Equal (pH and HCO3 go same way)
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Respiratory = Opposite (pH and CO2 go opposite ways). Classify pH first, then PaCO2/HCO3 to identify the primary process before selecting the intervention.
